title: Βορέας καὶ Ἥλιος
titleEn: The North Wind and the Sun
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 73 (= Perry 46). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Βορέας καὶ Ἥλιος περὶ δυνάμεως ἤριζον.
sentences.tr: Boreas kai Hēlios peri dynameōs ērizon.
sentences.l: North-Wind and Sun about power were-quarrelling.
sentences.i: The North Wind and the Sun were disputing which was the stronger.
sentences.n: The same frame as the Tortoise and the Hare's opening — X καὶ Y περὶ (genitive) ἤριζον. The fables reuse their machinery; you now own this sentence shape. Βορέας is the north wind personified, Ἥλιος the sun.

sentences.t: Ἔδοξε δὲ αὐτοῖς ἐκείνῳ τὴν νίκην ἀπονεῖμαι ὃς ἂν αὐτῶν ἄνθρωπον ὁδοιπόρον ἀποδύσῃ.
sentences.tr: Edoxe de autois ekeinōi tēn nikēn aponeimai hos an autōn anthrōpon hodoiporon apodysēi.
sentences.l: And it-seemed-good to-them to-that-one the victory to-assign whoever of-them a man wayfaring should-strip.
sentences.i: They agreed to award the victory to whichever of them could strip a traveler of his clothes.
sentences.n: ἔδοξε αὐτοῖς, 'it seemed good to them' = 'they resolved' — the standard decision formula (the same verb behind 'dogma'). ὃς ἄν + subjunctive (ἀποδύσῃ): 'whoever should…', the indefinite relative.

sentences.t: Καὶ ὁ Βορέας ἀρξάμενος σφοδρὸς ἦν·
sentences.tr: Kai ho Boreas arxamenos sphodros ēn:
sentences.l: And the North-Wind, having-begun, violent was:
sentences.i: The North Wind went first, and blew hard:

sentences.t: τοῦ δὲ ἀνθρώπου ἀντεχομένου τῆς ἐσθῆτος, μᾶλλον ἐπέκειτο.
sentences.tr: tou de anthrōpou antechomenou tēs esthētos, mallon epekeito.
sentences.l: and the man holding-fast to-the clothing, the-more he-pressed-on.
sentences.i: but the man only clutched his clothes tighter, so the wind bore down harder.
sentences.n: ἀντέχομαι + genitive: 'hold on to.' Another genitive absolute sets the scene; μᾶλλον ἐπέκειτο — escalation in two words.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ὑπὸ τοῦ ψύχους καταπονούμενος ἔτι μᾶλλον, καὶ περιττοτέραν ἐσθῆτα προσελάμβανεν, ἕως ἀποκαμὼν ⟨ὁ Βορέας⟩ τῷ Ἡλίῳ αὐτὸν παρέδωκε.
sentences.tr: Ho de hypo tou psychous kataponoumenos eti mallon, kai perittoteran esthēta proselambanen, heōs apokamōn ⟨ho Boreas⟩ tōi Hēliōi auton paredōke.
sentences.l: And he, by the cold worn-down still more, even additional clothing took-on-besides, until, having-grown-weary, ⟨the North-Wind⟩ to-the Sun him handed-over.
sentences.i: The colder the man got, the more layers he pulled on — until the North Wind, worn out, handed him over to the Sun.
sentences.n: The ⟨ὁ Βορέας⟩ is Chambry's editorial supplement — without it the sentence's subject silently switches from the man to the wind; the supplement makes the handover explicit.

sentences.t: Κἀκεῖνος τὸ μὲν πρῶτον μετρίως προσέλαμψε·
sentences.tr: Kakeinos to men prōton metriōs proselampse:
sentences.l: And-that-one at the first moderately shone-upon-him:
sentences.i: The Sun began by shining gently:
sentences.n: Κἀκεῖνος = καὶ ἐκεῖνος fused into one word — crasis, marked by the breathing sign inside the word (κἀ-). Greek does this contraction in writing, not just in speech.

sentences.t: τοῦ δὲ ἀνθρώπου τὰ περισσὰ τῶν ἱματίων ἀποτιθεμένου, σφοδρότερον τὸ καῦμα ἐπέτεινεν, ἕως οὗ πρὸς τὴν ἀλέαν ἀντέχειν μὴ δυνάμενος, ἀποδυσάμενος, ποταμοῦ παραρρέοντος ἐπὶ λουτρὸν ἀπῄει.
sentences.tr: tou de anthrōpou ta perissa tōn himatiōn apotithemenou, sphodroteron to kauma epeteinen, heōs hou pros tēn alean antechein mē dynamenos, apodysamenos, potamou pararreontos epi loutron apēiei.
sentences.l: and the man the superfluous of-the garments laying-aside, more-fiercely the heat he-intensified, until against the warmth to-hold-out not being-able, having-stripped-himself, a river flowing-past, to a bathe he-went-off.
sentences.i: As the man shed his extra garments, the Sun turned up the heat — until, unable to bear the warmth any longer, the man stripped off his clothes and went off to bathe in a river that ran nearby.
sentences.n: Three participial constructions stack up before the final verb ἀπῄει: a genitive absolute (the man shedding layers), a second genitive absolute (a river flowing past), and the man's own participles (μὴ δυνάμενος, ἀποδυσάμενος). The Greek sentence undresses him clause by clause.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι πολλάκις τὸ πείθειν τοῦ βιάζεσθαι ἀνυτικώτερόν ἐστι.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti pollakis to peithein tou biazesthai anytikōteron esti.
sentences.l: The story shows that often the persuading than-the using-force more-effective is.
sentences.i: The story shows that persuasion is often more effective than force.
sentences.n: Two articular infinitives face off: τὸ πείθειν, 'the persuading', against τοῦ βιάζεσθαι in the genitive of comparison — 'than forcing.' The whole moral is built from verbs turned into nouns.
